title: Reading the wider traffic picture
summary: Build a mental model of what other road users are likely to do next.

sections.heading: Scan in layers
sections.body: Look far ahead for the big picture (300 m on a motorway, the next junction in town), then mid-range for likely movement, then close in for immediate threats. Use the mirrors every few seconds to keep the picture complete behind you. A typical pattern is far → mid → near → mirror, repeated, with extra mirror checks before any change of speed or direction. Drivers who scan in a fixed rhythm miss far less than drivers who only look where the eye is drawn.

sections.heading: Predict, do not react
sections.body: A ball rolling into the road predicts a child. A delivery van with hazard lights predicts a door opening. Parked cars on a busy shopping street predict pedestrians stepping out. Spotting these signs early lets you adjust speed before you have to brake. The shift from 'I see — I brake' to 'I expect — I prepare' is what separates a hazard-aware driver from a merely competent one, and it is exactly the skill the hazard-perception part of the exam is designed to test.

sections.heading: Use the eyes of others
sections.body: Brake lights two or three cars ahead, dipped beams on at midday, an opened car door — these are all advance warnings. Use them to slow down before you can yet see the cause yourself. A cyclist turning their head, a pedestrian leaning forward at the kerb, a driver glancing into a mirror — all telegraph what comes next. Treat the road as a stream of information about other people's intentions, not just as a set of fixed obstacles.

sections.heading: Patterns and traps
sections.body: Some patterns repeat so often they are worth learning by heart. A queue in the next lane often hides a pedestrian stepping out between the stopped cars. A van or bus stopped ahead means someone may step out from behind it. A line of brake lights climbing toward you on the motorway means slow rapidly and switch on the hazard lights yourself once stopped. The classic 'second hazard' trap — fixating on the first risk and missing the second — is responsible for many serious crashes.

title: Anticipating vulnerable road users
summary: Where cyclists, pedestrians and motorcyclists tend to appear unexpectedly.

sections.heading: Cyclists in built-up areas
sections.body: Cyclists may swerve to avoid a pothole, an opening door, or a tram rail. They may turn left without a clear hand signal, especially older riders and children. Always leave space on the right and slow down before a junction where bikes can cross. Watch for the 'head-turn' over the shoulder — it almost always precedes a manoeuvre. Cargo bikes and bikes with child seats have a longer stopping distance and a wider swing through corners than they look like they should.

sections.heading: Children and elderly pedestrians
sections.body: Children are unpredictable and short — they may not see the road over a parked car. Elderly pedestrians may cross slowly or assume traffic will stop. Near schools, playgrounds and care homes, expect the unexpected. A child running along the pavement chasing another child is one of the highest-risk pedestrian patterns in town. Slow down before you see the problem, and make eye contact with anyone near the kerb.

sections.heading: Motorcyclists in your blind spot
sections.body: Motorcyclists are easy to miss in a brief mirror glance — they often sit in the blind spot for several seconds. Before changing lane on a motorway or before turning left across oncoming traffic, do an extra mirror plus over-the-shoulder check. Riders may filter between queues of cars in stop-and-go traffic; check both mirrors and the gap before opening a door or drifting toward the lane edge. Their narrow profile also makes their speed harder to judge — what looks like a slow distant dot may be a fast bike about to arrive.

sections.heading: Right-turn and parked-car traps
sections.body: The 'right hook' — turning right across a cyclist who is going straight on alongside you — is one of the most dangerous urban manoeuvres for cyclists. Before turning right, check the mirror, glance over the right shoulder, and only turn when you can see the cycle path is clear. The 'dooring' risk — opening a car door into a passing cyclist — is best managed with the so-called Dutch reach: open with your far hand, which naturally turns your body and head to look behind. A bus or lorry's right blind spot is large; never sit alongside one at a junction.

title: Weather, surface and visibility hazards
summary: Subtle changes in conditions that demand earlier action.

sections.heading: First rain after dry weather
sections.body: After a dry spell, the first rain lifts oil and dust to the surface — grip drops sharply for the first 10–15 minutes. Increase following distance and brake earlier. Painted road markings, manhole covers, and tram rails become particularly slippery in this window, especially for two-wheelers. Standing water on a long straight raises the risk of aquaplaning above about 80 km/h; if the steering goes light, lift off the throttle and steer straight rather than braking.

sections.heading: Black ice and shaded patches
sections.body: Bridges, tunnel exits, and shaded forest roads freeze first. If the dashboard shows close to 0 °C, treat shadows and bridges as potentially icy even if the rest of the road looks dry. Black ice is invisible — it gives the road a faintly shiny, wet look without splash. If you suspect ice, brake gently in a straight line and avoid sudden steering. Early winter mornings after a clear night are the highest-risk time, even when the air is well above freezing.

sections.heading: Sun glare and low light
sections.body: Low sun in winter and at dawn or dusk blinds you and the drivers facing you. Pedestrians and cyclists can disappear into the glare, especially against a wet road that mirrors the sun back at you. Slow down, use the sun visor and clean the windscreen — a dirty screen scatters the glare. A pair of polarised sunglasses helps cut reflected glare from wet tarmac, but make sure you can still read instrument displays through them.

sections.heading: Wind, debris, and seasonal hazards
sections.body: Strong side winds on a bridge, open polder, or motorway exit can push a light car or a high-sided vehicle sideways — both hands on the wheel and an early gap to the lane edge let you correct without overreacting. After a storm, watch for branches, wet leaves, and gravel on bends, all of which sharply reduce grip. In autumn, wet leaves on shaded asphalt are nearly as slippery as ice; in spring, mud and tractor tracks on rural roads are the equivalent. The memory hook: 'if it covers the road, treat it like ice'.

title: Decision-making under pressure
summary: How to choose the safest option when a hazard becomes a real risk.

sections.heading: Speed is your reserve
sections.body: Most emergencies are solved by having a few km/h in hand. If something looks unsettled ahead — a child near the kerb, a wobbly cyclist, a stationary vehicle on the hard shoulder — lift off in time. The cost of arriving two seconds later is nothing; the cost of a collision is enormous. Defensive drivers think of speed as a 'budget' that they keep some of in reserve, rather than spending all the way up to the limit.

sections.heading: Brake or steer?
sections.body: If a hazard appears directly in your path and you have time, brake straight. Swerving on a wet road or at speed can lose grip and put you into oncoming traffic. Only steer to avoid if braking will not be enough and there is a clear, safe space to steer into. Modern ABS lets you keep steering control while braking hard — keep both hands on the wheel and look where you want the car to go, not at the obstacle itself.

sections.heading: Mind the second hazard
sections.body: Many accidents happen because the driver fixed on the first hazard and missed the second — for example, braking for a cyclist and being rear-ended by the car behind, or swerving around debris into the next lane and meeting an overtaking motorbike. Keep scanning even while reacting. As a rule of thumb: after the brake, the next glance is the mirror, then forward again — 'react, check, plan'.

sections.heading: After the close call
sections.body: When you have had a near miss, the adrenaline keeps your reactions sharp but also narrows your attention. Slow down a little, breathe out, and reset the scan pattern — that is exactly the moment a second incident is most likely. If you are genuinely shaken, find a safe place to stop for a few minutes before continuing. The most experienced drivers treat a near miss as free training: ask 'what cue did I miss, and how would I spot it earlier next time?', and the same close call is less likely to find you again.
